nodes.description: Accumulation of ALA in acute hepatic porphyria causes acute neurovisceral attacks, including abdominal pain, peripheral neuropathy, muscle weakness, and sometimes respiratory insufficiency. ALAD porphyria lacks primary skin symptoms.

evidence.reference_title: Case Report: Lack of Response to Givosiran in a Case of ALAD Porphyria.
evidence.snippet: Introduction: 5-Aminolevulinic acid dehydratase (ALAD) porphyria (ADP) is an autosomal recessive disease characterized by a profound deficiency in ALAD, the second enzyme in the heme biosynthetic pathway, and acute neurovisceral attacks with abdominal pain and peripheral neuropathy.
A rare acute hepatic porphyria characterized by neurovisceral attacks without skin symptoms.

evidence.reference_title: Porphyric neuropathy.
evidence.snippet: Acute hepatic porphyrias are inherited metabolic disorders that may present with polyneuropathy, which if not diagnosed early can lead to quadriparesis, respiratory weakness, and death.

nodes.description: ALAD, also known as porphobilinogen synthase, normally condenses two ALA molecules to form PBG in the cytosolic part of the heme biosynthetic pathway. Profound ALAD deficiency blocks this second heme-biosynthesis step.

evidence.reference_title: Case Report: Lack of Response to Givosiran in a Case of ALAD Porphyria.
evidence.snippet: Introduction: 5-Aminolevulinic acid dehydratase (ALAD) porphyria (ADP) is an autosomal recessive disease characterized by a profound deficiency in ALAD, the second enzyme in the heme biosynthetic pathway, and acute neurovisceral attacks with abdominal pain and peripheral neuropathy.

evidence.reference_title: ALAD porphyria is a conformational disease.
evidence.snippet: PBGS, also called "delta-aminolevulinate dehydratase," is encoded by the ALAD gene and catalyzes the second step in the biosynthesis of heme.

nodes.description: Pathogenic ALAD variants can impair porphobilinogen synthase by destabilizing the active octameric enzyme, shifting the morpheein equilibrium toward low-activity hexamers, or producing unstable or low-activity mutant proteins. The result is profound residual ALAD activity loss in affected patients.
nodes.id: ALAD conformational and biallelic variant defects

evidence.reference_title: ALAD porphyria is a conformational disease.
evidence.snippet: Thus, all porphyria-associated human PBGS variants are found to shift the morpheein equilibrium for PBGS toward the less active hexamer.

evidence.reference_title: delta-Aminolevulinate dehydratase (ALAD) porphyria: the first case in North America with two novel ALAD mutations.
evidence.snippet: These results suggest that the combination of the two aberrant ALADs with little enzyme activity accounts for the markedly decreased ALAD activity observed in the proband.

evidence.reference_title: Novel molecular defects of the delta-aminolevulinate dehydratase gene in a patient with inherited acute hepatic porphyria.
evidence.snippet: These data thus demonstrate that the proband was associated with 2 novel molecular defects of the ALAD gene, 1 in each allele, and account for the extremely low ALAD activity in his erythrocytes ( approximately 1% of normal).

evidence.reference_title: delta-Aminolevulinate dehydratase (ALAD) porphyria: the first case in North America with two novel ALAD mutations.
evidence.snippet: This case represents the molecular analysis of the ALAD gene defects in the first case of ADP identified in North America, who is a compound heterozygote for two novel ALAD gene defects.

evidence.reference_title: New cases of delta-aminolevulinic acid dehydratase deficiency: Functional insights into gene variants using an innovative mouse liver model.
evidence.snippet: These findings underscore the molecular heterogeneity of the ALAD gene and present the first reported case of ADP in a female patient.

evidence.reference_title: ALAD / porphyria due to ALA dehydratase deficiency (Moderate)
evidence.snippet: ALAD | HGNC:395 | porphyria due to ALA dehydratase deficiency | MONDO:0013000 | AR | Moderate

nodes.description: Abdominal pain is a frequent neurovisceral manifestation during acute ADP attacks.

evidence.snippet: HP:0002027 | Abdominal pain | Frequent (79-30%)

evidence.reference_title: Case Report: Lack of Response to Givosiran in a Case of ALAD Porphyria.
evidence.snippet: Introduction: 5-Aminolevulinic acid dehydratase (ALAD) porphyria (ADP) is an autosomal recessive disease characterized by a profound deficiency in ALAD, the second enzyme in the heme biosynthetic pathway, and acute neurovisceral attacks with abdominal pain and peripheral neuropathy.

nodes.description: Abnormal circulating or excreted porphyrin-pathway intermediates are a frequent biochemical phenotype.

evidence.snippet: HP:0010472 | Abnormal circulating porphyrin concentration | Frequent (79-30%)

nodes.description: Constipation is one of the gastrointestinal symptoms reported in ADP.

evidence.snippet: HP:0002019 | Constipation | Occasional (29-5%)

evidence.reference_title: Novel molecular defects of the delta-aminolevulinate dehydratase gene in a patient with inherited acute hepatic porphyria.
evidence.snippet: These data thus demonstrate that the proband was associated with 2 novel molecular defects of the ALAD gene, 1 in each allele, and account for the extremely low ALAD activity in his erythrocytes ( approximately 1% of normal).

readouts.interpretation: Markedly reduced erythrocyte ALAD activity directly reports the deficient porphobilinogen synthase step in heme biosynthesis.

nodes.description: ADP is classified as an acute hepatic porphyria, and patient data show elevated circulating hepatic ALAS1 mRNA. Hepatic ALAS1 induction increases upstream pathway flux and supports the liver as an important source of excess ALA, although erythroid marrow may also contribute.
nodes.id: Hepatic ALAS1 induction and excess ALA production

evidence.reference_title: 5-Aminolevulinate dehydratase porphyria: Update on hepatic 5-aminolevulinic acid synthase induction and long-term response to hemin.
evidence.snippet: RESULTS: Circulating hepatic 5-aminolevulinic acid synthase-1 (ALAS1) mRNA was elevated in this case, as in other AHPs.

evidence.reference_title: 5-Aminolevulinate dehydratase porphyria: Update on hepatic 5-aminolevulinic acid synthase induction and long-term response to hemin.
evidence.snippet: Elevation in circulating hepatic ALAS1 and response to treatment with hemin indicate that the liver is an important source of excess ALA in ADP, although the marrow may also contribute.

nodes.description: Because ALAD lies upstream of PBG, affected patients excrete large amounts of urinary ALA while lacking the PBG overproduction seen in acute intermittent porphyria. Total porphyrins, coproporphyrin III, and protoporphyrin abnormalities can also be present, contributing to abnormal porphyrin laboratory profiles and dark or purple urine.
nodes.id: Hepatic and erythroid ALA accumulation without PBG overproduction

evidence.reference_title: ALAD porphyria.
evidence.snippet: Because of an almost complete lack of ALAD activity, patients excrete a large amount of ALA, but not PBG, into urine.

evidence.reference_title: 5-Aminolevulinic acid dehydratase deficiency porphyria: a twenty-year clinical and biochemical follow-up.
evidence.snippet: After this therapy both urinary 5-aminolevulinic acid (ALA) and total porphyrins were diminished to 65% in patient B.

evidence.reference_title: Investigations on the formation of urinary coproporphyrin isomers I-IV in 5-aminolevulinic acid dehydratase deficiency porphyria, acute lead intoxication and after oral 5-aminolevulinic acid loading.
evidence.snippet: The concentration of total coproporphyrins was about 30-fold increased in patients with ALAD deficiency porphyria and acute lead intoxication as compared with controls.

nodes.description: Increased erythrocyte protoporphyrin concentration is one of the porphyrin pathway laboratory abnormalities recorded for ADP.

nodes.description: Fecal coproporphyrin 3 elevation is a porphyrin-profile abnormality reported for ADP.

evidence.snippet: HP:0033010 | Increased fecal coproporphyrin 3 | Frequent (79-30%)

nodes.description: Intravenous hemin or heme arginate is the best documented attack-directed therapy in ADP. Clinical benefit supports hepatic ALAS1 repression and reduction of ALA production as the main therapeutic mechanism.
nodes.id: Intravenous hemin or heme arginate for acute attacks

evidence.reference_title: 5-Aminolevulinate dehydratase porphyria: Update on hepatic 5-aminolevulinic acid synthase induction and long-term response to hemin.
evidence.snippet: Intravenous hemin was effective in most reported cases for treatment and prevention of acute attacks of neurological symptoms.

evidence.reference_title: Case Report: Lack of Response to Givosiran in a Case of ALAD Porphyria.
evidence.snippet: Hemin infusions are often effective in treating and preventing such attacks.

nodes.description: Muscle weakness is a frequent manifestation of the neurologic attack phenotype.

nodes.description: Purple or dark urine reflects accumulation and oxidation of porphyrin-pathway intermediates during attacks.

evidence.snippet: The patients' enzyme activity was <10% from 1977 to 1997.
